message.content: I have a problem... when I run this url https://api.ixxdna.com/rd/user/change-password I am receiving a cors error in the browser, however the operation is OK, el container do teh upfdate and return 200. This error is happening only on prod, because en dev is not happening https://api-dev.ixxdna.com/rd/user/change-password the source for dev is in /opt/development/git/shokworks/intellxxdna/terraform/intell-terraform-infra-azure/environments/dev

content.thinking: Let me investigate the CORS configuration differences between prod and dev environments for this API endpoint.

content.text: Let me compare the CORS configuration between dev and prod environments.

##=============================================================================
## PostgreSQL Database Configuration
##=============================================================================
## Required for Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server deployment
## 
## IMPORTANT: Change these values before deploying to production!
## Password requirements:
## - 8-128 characters
## - Must contain uppercase letters, lowercase letters, numbers, special characters
##
## These credentials are stored in the tfstate file which contains sensitive data
## Ensure proper access controls on the remote state storage account

postgres_admin_username = "postgres"          # CHANGE THIS to a secure username
postgres_admin_password = "DevPassword123!@#" # CHANGE THIS to a secure password

# User database for intellxxdna
intellxxdna_admin_username = "intellxx-admin"
intellxxdna_admin_password = "tGo8iHOMgsB"



##=============================================================================
## Container Apps - ACR Configuration
##=============================================================================
## Shared Container Registry credentials for pulling images
## These values are used to authenticate with ixdnaacrshared.azurecr.io
##
## To get ACR credentials:
## az acr credential show --name ixdnaacrshared --resource-group rg-intellxxdna-lexxi-shared

# Shared ACR configuration
shared_container_registry_login_server = "ixdnaacrshared.azurecr.io"
shared_container_registry_username     = "a6c84cf5-4e36-4daa-af59-07fffc61f803"     # ARM_CLIENT_ID
shared_container_registry_password     = "MTV8Q~2x5G7j_N4E4zM1vg2CY5xW5IMuW694IbhG" # ARM_CLIENT_SECRET



##=============================================================================
## Static Web App Configuration
##=============================================================================
## Azure Static Web App for Angular frontend deployment
## GitHub Enterprise integration with PAT token authentication
## Deployment: Automatic on push to develop branch
## Build: ng build → dist/
## Cost: Free tier (no monthly charge)
##
## GitHub Token Setup:
## 1. Go to GitHub Enterprise Settings → Developer settings → Personal access tokens
## 2. Create new token with scopes: admin:repo_hook, repo (full), workflow
## 3. Set the token value via command line or environment variable:
##    terraform apply -var="github_token=ghp_xxxxx..."
##    OR
##    export TF_VAR_github_token="ghp_xxxxx..."
##

lexxi_github_repo_url = "https://intellxxdna.ghe.com/IXXD/intell-lexxi-frontend"
rd_github_repo_url    = "https://intellxxdna.ghe.com/IXXD/snp-data-cleansing-frontend"
github_token          = "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"



##=============================================================================
## Keycloak IAM Configuration
##=============================================================================
## Identity and Access Management service configuration
## Keycloak provides enterprise-grade OIDC/OAuth2, LDAP/AD integration,
## and user/realm/client management
##
## IMPORTANT: Change admin password to a secure value before production!
## Password requirements:
## - 8+ characters
## - Recommended: Mix of uppercase, lowercase, numbers, special characters
##
## These credentials are stored in the tfstate file which contains sensitive data
## Ensure proper access controls on the remote state storage account

keycloak_admin_username = "intell-admin"
keycloak_admin_password = "Dcd16cAz329ng?I"



##=============================================================================
## RD CONFIGURATION
##=============================================================================
## Only used in dev environment for private database access
##

rd_swagger_username = "Bony7239"

rd_swagger_pw = "1#s7&X0HllW#"

rd_environment = "development"

rd_jwt_secret_key = "u;&D£H?v\">9Y35D~Jj4CnY^Rps'9%Yh08£H5wsYoY':;@eW<4>"

rd_cors_allow_origins = "http://localhost:5137,http://localhost:3000,http://localhost:8080,http://127.0.0.1:3000,https://lexxi-az-dev.ixxdna.com,https://rnd-dev.ixxdna.com"

rd_access_token_expire_minutes = "7200"

rd_sentry_dsn = "https://6088d4ae374cc6c85d9841d1dfc352a8@o4511309635452928.ingest.us.sentry.io/4511314944983040"



##=============================================================================
## Lexxi CONFIGURATION
##=============================================================================
## Only used in dev environment for private database access
##

lexxi_openai_token = "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"

lexxi_cors_allow_origins = "http://localhost:5137,http://localhost:3000,http://localhost:8080,http://127.0.0.1:3000,https://lexxi-az-dev.ixxdna.com,https://rnd-dev.ixxdna.com"

lexxi_sentry_dsn = "https://9d7a3b5a28b666cebb8cd2c60e93cd93@o4511309635452928.ingest.us.sentry.io/4511314825117696"



##=============================================================================
## MLFlow CONFIGURATION
##=============================================================================

## Authentication access
mlflow_admin_username = "admin"
mlflow_admin_password = "lGo6iH10MavP"

## Tracing setup.
mlflow_tracking_uri    = "https://api-dev.ixxdna.com/mlflow/"
mlflow_allowed_origins = "https://api-dev.ixxdna.com"

mlflow_tracing_enabled = true

##=============================================================================
## PostgreSQL Database Configuration

container_lexxi_app_image_name = "lexxi"

##=============================================================================
## Azure Front Door Configuration
##=============================================================================
## Public-facing CDN with managed HTTPS (*.azurefd.net certificate)
## Standard SKU: Cost-optimized for dev (~$6/month including WAF and DDoS)
## WAF Mode: Detection (logs threats, safe for testing)
## Features:

## - Global CDN with automatic failover
## - DDoS protection (layer 3-7)
## - Built-in health probes

front_door_sku                = "Standard_AzureFrontDoor" # ~$6/month, includes WAF and CDN
enable_front_door_waf         = true

**Dev (line 97):**
```
rd_cors_allow_origins = "http://localhost:5137,http://localhost:3000,http://localhost:8080,http://127.0.0.1:3000,https://lexxi-az-dev.ixxdna.com,https://rnd-dev.ixxdna.com"
```

**Prod (line 136):**
```
rd_cors_allow_origins = "https://lexxi.ixxdna.com,https://rnd.ixxdna.com"


```

The prod configuration only allows those two specific origins, but the user is hitting the API endpoint from a different origin that's not in the allowlist, which is triggering the CORS error.

```
But actually, the issue is that the browser request is getting CORS error only on prod.

Looking at the CORS origins for prod:
- `https://lexxi.ixxdna.com`
- `https://rnd.ixxdna.com`

The user might be hitting the change-password endpoint from another origin that's not in this list. But actually, the frontend for the "rd" service should be at `https://rnd.ixxdna.com` - that should be allowed.

Wait - the user says the operation succeeds (200), but there's a CORS error. This is interesting. This can happen when:
1. The preflight OPTIONS request succeeds but the CORS headers are missing from the actual response
2. The CORS headers are present in the request but not correct for the origin making the request

Actually wait - if the container returns 200 and the operation completes, but CORS error appears in the browser... this might be a case where the request is simple enough to skip preflight, so it goes through and the server processes it, but the response lacks the proper `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` header, causing the browser to block it even though the operation already completed. Or Azure Front Door could be involved, potentially stripping CORS headers or having its own CORS configuration.

Let me check the main.tf files to see how CORS is configured and whether Azure Front Door or API Management is in the mix. I suspect the real issue is that the user is accessing the change-password endpoint from an origin not in the prod allowlist - only `https://lexxi.ixxdna.com` and `https://rnd.ixxdna.com` are permitted.
``` which dev doesn't have. The Azure Front Door and its WAF could be stripping or modifying the CORS headers in production. Let me check the main.tf configuration to see what's actually being set up differently between environments.

The custom domains in prod are `api.ixxdna.com` (API via Front Door), `lexxi.ixxdna.com` (frontend), and `rnd.ixxdna.com` (frontend).

So the allowed origins for the RD backend are only `https://lexxi.ixxdna.com` and `https://rnd.ixxdna.com`.


A recent commit added OIDC authentication with Keycloak and configured automatic redirects for unauthenticated users, which could mean the user is being redirected through an IdP when accessing the password change endpoint. The user reports hitting `https://api.ixxdna.com/rd/user/change-password` and getting a CORS error despite a 200 response—this is the classic scenario where the browser blocks the response due to missing CORS headers, even though the request succeeded on the server.

The issue is likely that the origin making the request (possibly from Keycloak's account management pages or a different domain) isn't in the production CORS allowlist. I need to check what Keycloak URL is configured in the prod environment to see if that origin needs to be added.

content.thinking:
I can see that Keycloak is at `/auth` path. So Keycloak's URL would be `https://api.ixxdna.com/auth`. If the user is on a Keycloak-hosted page (e.g., the account management pages), the origin would be `https://api.ixxdna.com`.

But actually, a request from `https://api.ixxdna.com/auth/...` to `https://api.ixxdna.com/rd/user/change-password` would be a same-origin request (both are on `https://api.ixxdna.com`), so CORS wouldn't apply.
 
Actually, I think the real scenario is that the frontend at `https://rnd.ixxdna.com` is making the request to `https://api.ixxdna.com/rd/user/change-password`, and while that domain should be in the CORS allowlist, the running container might not have the latest Terraform changes applied yet, or there's another configuration issue preventing it from working.

But wait—the user mentioned the operation returns 200, which means the backend is processing it successfully. If CORS is blocking the response, that's happening after the request completes, which suggests either the response is missing the `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` header or Azure Front Door has its own CORS policies that are overriding the backend's headers.

For a POST request with a JSON body like a password change, the browser would normally send a preflight OPTIONS request first, so if that's failing while the actual POST succeeds, that points to a mismatch in how CORS is configured between the preflight and the actual request handling.

Looking at the CORS allowlist and the recent commits about migrating from SAML to OIDC, I'm wondering if the issue is that a legacy .NET application at a different origin—maybe the old portal—is trying to call the RD API for password changes, but its origin isn't in the allowlist. The commit mentions adding OIDC clients, but the specific origins involved aren't clear, so I should dig into what those are.

valid_redirect_uris = [
    "https://localhost/*"
  ]

  ##=======================================================================
  ## WEB ORIGINS (CORS - Cross-Origin Resource Sharing)
  ##=======================================================================
  ## web_origins = [...]
  ##   - Whitelisted origins allowed to make CORS requests to Keycloak
  ##   - Prevents malicious websites from stealing user tokens
  ##   - Only allows JavaScript from known origins to access Keycloak tokens
  ##   - Must omit path (CORS is enforced at origin level, not path level)
  ##   - Typically same as your frontend domain
  ##
  ##   Security Impact:
  ##   - Without CORS restriction: Any website can make requests to Keycloak on user's behalf
  ##   - With CORS restriction: Only your frontend domain can access user tokens/endpoints
  ##
  web_origins = [
    "https://localhost"
  ]

  depends_on = [
    keycloak_realm.main
  ]
}

##=============================================================================
## KEYCLOAK OPENID CLIENT - LEXXI BACKEND API
##=============================================================================
## Registers the Lexxi backend API as a confidential OAuth2/OpenID Connect client
## This client represents server-to-server or internal API communication
##
## Client Type: CONFIDENTIAL (Backend API)
## Authentication: Client credentials flow (for service-to-service auth)
## Purpose: Secure API-to-API communication with client secret authentication
##
## Security Notes:
## - Confidential clients can securely store secrets (runs on secure server)
## - Uses client_id + client_secret for authentication (like Basic Auth)
## - Service accounts enable service-to-service authentication
## - Tokens include 'aud' (audience) claim to restrict token usage to specific APIs
##
## Communication Pattern:
## 1. Lexxi API → Keycloak: POST /token with client_id + client_secret
## 2. Keycloak → Lexxi API: Returns JWT with audience claim
## 3. Frontend/other services: Can verify token is meant for this API via 'aud' claim
##

resource "keycloak_openid_client" "lexxi_backend_api" {

  realm_id  = keycloak_realm.main.id
  client_id = "lexxi-backend-api-client"
  name      = "Lexxi Backend API Client"
  enabled   = true

  ##=======================================================================
  ## CLIENT AUTHENTICATION SETTINGS
  ##=======================================================================
  ## access_type = "CONFIDENTIAL"
  ##   - Indicates this is a confidential client (backend server)
  ##   - Client can securely store and use a secret
  ##   - Uses client credentials or authorization code + secret for auth
  ##
  ## service_accounts_enabled = true
  ##   - Allows API to authenticate on its own (service-to-service auth)
  ##   - Without this: API can only be called with user-delegated tokens
  ##   - With this: API can obtain tokens directly for internal operations
  ##   - Use case: Batch jobs, scheduled tasks, inter-service calls
  ##
  access_type              = "CONFIDENTIAL"
  service_accounts_enabled = true

  depends_on = [
    keycloak_openid_client.lexxi_web_app_client
  ]
}

##=============================================================================
## KEYCLOAK OPENID AUDIENCE PROTOCOL MAPPER - LEXXI BACKEND API
##=============================================================================
## Injects the 'aud' (Audience) claim into JWT tokens issued to the Lexxi
## Web App client, so the Lexxi Backend API can verify the token was intended
## specifically for it.
##
## Why is this needed?
## ├─ Without an audience claim, a token issued for any client could technically
## │  be sent to any API — nothing stops token reuse across services
## ├─ With this mapper, every token issued to the frontend includes:
## │  "aud": "lexxi-backend-api"
## └─ The Backend API validates this claim and rejects tokens meant for others
##
## Where it is attached:
## ├─ Client: lexxi-web-app-client (the Angular SPA)
## └─ Effect: every token the frontend obtains will carry the audience claim
##
## Security Flow:
## 1. User logs in via the Angular SPA → Keycloak issues JWT
## 2. JWT contains: { "aud": "lexxi-backend-api", "sub": "user-uuid", ... }
## 3. Angular sends JWT to the Backend API
## 4. Backend API checks: aud == "lexxi-backend-api" ✓ → request accepted
## 5. If same token is sent to a different API:
##    └─ That API checks: aud != "its-own-id" ✗ → request rejected
##
## Mapper Type: Audience (aud claim)
## Attached to: lexxi-web-app-client
## Audience value injected: "lexxi-backend-api"
##

resource "keycloak_openid_audience_protocol_mapper" "api_audience_mapper" {

  realm_id  = keycloak_realm.main.id
  client_id = keycloak_openid_client.lexxi_web_app_client.id
  name      = "lexxi-backend-api-audience-mapper"

  ##=======================================================================
  ## AUDIENCE CONFIGURATION
  ##=======================================================================
  ## included_custom_audience = "lexxi-backend-api"
  ##   - The audience value to include in JWT tokens
  ##   - Must match the Lexxi API's expected audience
  ##   - When frontend requests token, it will include this audience claim
  ##   - Lexxi API can then verify the token is intended for it
  ##
  ##   Example JWT:
  ##   {
  ##     "iss": "https://keycloak.example.com/auth/realms/lexxi",
  ##     "sub": "user-uuid",
  ##     "aud": "lexxi-backend-api",     ← This claim was added by mapper
  ##     "iat": 1609459200,
  ##     "exp": 1609462800
  ##   }
  ##
  included_custom_audience = "lexxi-backend-api"

  depends_on = [
    keycloak_openid_client.lexxi_web_app_client,
    keycloak_openid_client.lexxi_backend_api
  ]
}

##
## Dev Environment - Main Configuration
## Author: Raul Peña (rpena@shokworks.io)
## Purpose: Orchestrates all infrastructure modules for the development environment
##
## Environment: Development (cost-optimized, single-region)
##
## Modules & Resources Deployed:
## - Keycloak: Authentication and identity management


##=============================================================================
## CURRENT AZURE CONTEXT
##=============================================================================
## Get current subscription and tenant information for identity and RBAC configuration

data "azurerm_client_config" "current" {}


##=============================================================================
## KEYCLOAK RESOURCES
##=============================================================================

##=============================================================================
## KEYCLOAK REALM - MAIN
##=============================================================================
## Creates the primary realm for Lexxi Keycloak authentication
## Realms are isolated authentication domains where users, clients, and roles exist
##

resource "keycloak_realm" "main" {

  realm        = "IntellxxDNA"
  enabled      = true
  display_name = "IntellxxDNA Realm"

  # Password policy: minimum 12 chars, 1 uppercase, 1 lowercase, 1 digit, 1 special char
  password_policy = "length(12) and upperCase(1) and lowerCase(1) and digits(1) and specialChars(1)"
}

##=============================================================================
## KEYCLOAK OPENID CLIENT - LEXXI WEB APP
##=============================================================================
## Registers the Lexxi frontend as a public OAuth2/OpenID Connect client
## This client handles user authentication for the Lexxi single-page application
##
## Client Type: PUBLIC
## Authentication Flow: Authorization Code Flow with PKCE (OAuth 2.0 standard)
## Purpose: Secure browser-based authentication without exposing client secrets
##
## Security Notes:
## - Public clients cannot securely store secrets (runs in user's browser)
## - PKCE (RFC 7636) prevents authorization code interception attacks
## - Redirect URIs must be explicitly whitelisted to prevent open redirects
## - CORS origins limited to prevent unauthorized token requests
##

resource "keycloak_openid_client" "lexxi_web_app_client" {

  realm_id  = keycloak_realm.main.id
  client_id = "lexxi-web-app-client"
  name      = "Lexxi Web App Client"
  enabled   = true

  ##=======================================================================
  ## CLIENT AUTHENTICATION & FLOW SETTINGS
  ##=======================================================================
  ## access_type = "PUBLIC"
  ##   - Indicates this is a public client (browser-based SPA)
  ##   - Client cannot authenticate with a secret
  ##   - Used for single-page applications, mobile apps, desktop clients
  ##
  ## standard_flow_enabled = true
  ##   - Enables Authorization Code Flow (recommended for SPAs)
  ##   - User redirects to Keycloak login → receives auth code → app exchanges for token
  ##   - More secure than implicit flow (flow no longer recommended)
  ##
  access_type           = "PUBLIC"
  standard_flow_enabled = true

  ##=======================================================================
  ## PKCE (PROOF KEY FOR AUTHORIZATION CODE EXCHANGE)
  ##=======================================================================
  ## pkce_code_challenge_method = "S256"
  ##   - PKCE adds extra security layer to Authorization Code Flow
  ##   - Prevents authorization code interception/replay attacks
  ##   - Client generates random code_verifier → sends code_challenge (SHA256 hash)
  ##   - When exchanging code for token, client proves it has original code_verifier
  ##   - Mandatory security requirement for public clients in production (2026+ standards)
  ##   - Options: "S256" (SHA256, recommended) or "plain" (not recommended)
  ##
  pkce_code_challenge_method = "S256"

  ##=======================================================================
  ## REDIRECT URIS (Authorization Code Response Destinations)
  ##=======================================================================
  ## valid_redirect_uris = [...]
  ##   - Whitelisted URLs where Keycloak can redirect after user login
  ##   - Prevents open redirect attacks (attacker cannot redirect to arbitrary URLs)
  ##   - Must match exactly (protocol, domain, path) - wildcards allowed only on paths
  ##   - Azure Static Web App: automatically generates *.azurestaticapps.net domain
  ##   - Wildcard pattern: "https://domain/*" allows all paths under domain
  ##
  ##   Example redirects that will be allowed:
  ##   - https://myapp.azurestaticapps.net/
  ##   - https://myapp.azurestaticapps.net/dashboard
  ##   - https://myapp.azurestaticapps.net/auth/callback
  ##
  valid_redirect_uris = [

content.thinking:
I see that in the prod addons, the `lexxi_web_app_client` only has `"https://localhost/*"` as valid_redirect_uris and `"https://localhost"` as web_origins. This seems like the prod addons were not fully configured yet.

Now let me check what the change-password scenario is:
1. The `rd_cors_allow_origins` in prod is `"https://lexxi.ixxdna.com,https://rnd.ixxdna.com"`

But the recent commit added a OIDC identity provider for a legacy .NET app. The legacy .NET app might be at some other domain. 

So the user is likely hitting `https://api.ixxdna.com/rd/user/change-password` from either the Keycloak account management page at `https://api.ixxdna.com/auth` or from the legacy portal, and the browser is throwing a CORS error because the calling origin isn't in the allowed list.

Actually, wait - if the request is coming from `rnd.ixxdna.com`, that origin should be in the production CORS configuration. The real issue might be that the preflight OPTIONS request is failing or returning without the proper CORS headers, or the actual response is missing `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` and `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials` headers. Since the user mentions the container does complete the update and return a 200, the backend is processing the request, but the browser is still blocking the response because the CORS headers aren't being sent back correctly. The CORS configuration shows different allowed origins for dev and prod environments, so I need to figure out which domain is actually making the request to the password change endpoint. I'm wondering if there's a legacy frontend domain that isn't in the current CORS allowlist, or if the recent `feature/kc_target` branch removed some origins that should still be there.

content.thinking:
Hmm, the prod tfvars might be gitignored. Let me check if there's a .gitignore.

Actually let me take a step back. The problem is clear from what I've already read:

**Prod `rd_cors_allow_origins`:**
```
rd_cors_allow_origins = "https://lexxi.ixxdna.com,https://rnd.ixxdna.com"
```

These are the only two origins allowed. But the user is hitting the endpoint and getting a CORS error. The question is: what origin is the request coming from?
 
The most likely scenario is that the user's request is coming from a different origin that isn't in the prod allowlist. Given the recent commit mentioning a legacy .NET application and the change-password feature being triggered, there's probably an older portal URL like `https://portal.ixxdna.com` or `https://app.ixxdna.com` that needs to be added to the CORS configuration.

content.thinking:
Now I can see the full picture:

**Production architecture:**
- API: `https://api.ixxdna.com` (Azure Front Door → Container Apps)
- Lexxi frontend: `https://lexxi.ixxdna.com` (Azure Static Web App)
- RD frontend: `https://rnd.ixxdna.com` (Azure Static Web App)

**RD CORS allowed origins in prod:** `"https://lexxi.ixxdna.com,https://rnd.ixxdna.com"`

Both frontend domains are in the CORS list... So why is there a CORS error?
 
Actually, wait. The user might be hitting this endpoint from within Keycloak's account management at `https://api.ixxdna.com/auth`, which would be same-origin and wouldn't trigger CORS. But more likely, the change-password feature is being called from the RD frontend at `rnd.ixxdna.com` to the API at `api.ixxdna.com`, so the Origin header would be `https://rnd.ixxdna.com` — which should be allowed based on the CORS config.

But here's what's strange: the request succeeds with a 200 response, yet the CORS error still appears. This happens when the response is missing the `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` header, or when credentials are included but the header is set to `*`, or when Azure Front Door is stripping the CORS headers entirely. Given that this only happens in production, Azure Front Door is probably the culprit — it's likely intercepting the CORS preflight and either not forwarding it properly or removing the CORS headers from the backend response.

The fix would be to configure Azure Front Door to preserve CORS headers through response header rules, or ensure the backend is handling CORS correctly even when behind AFD. Interestingly, dev also has Front Door configured, so I need to check whether the dev environment is actually routing through it or if there's a difference in how the custom domain is set up between environments.

Looking at the prod tfvars, I see `enable_custom_domain_to_rd` and `enable_custom_domain_to_lexxi` are both true, but I don't see these flags in the dev tfvars. This suggests that in dev, traffic might be going directly to the Container App URL rather than through the custom domain at AFD, which would explain why CORS works there. In prod, all traffic routes through `api.ixxdna.com` via AFD, and that's likely where the CORS headers are being stripped.

Let me check if the Front Door module has any response header rules configured for CORS...

Searching through the Front Door module...

Now I'm seeing the actual issue: the request succeeds on the server side (200 response), but the browser still blocks it because the response lacks the proper CORS headers. This can happen in two scenarios—either the browser sends a simple request without preflight and the server responds without `Access-Control-Allow-Origin`, or the preflight OPTIONS request passes but the actual request fails to include the required headers in its response.

Let me examine the Front Door configuration more thoroughly to see if there are caching policies or response header rules that might be stripping out the CORS headers.

   165→

   176→  cdn_frontdoor_rule_set_id = azurerm_cdn_frontdoor_rule_set.strip_prefix_rules[0].id
   177→  # Order is alphabetical by name to ensure deterministic ordering

   179→  behavior_on_match = "Continue"

content.thinking:
Now I have a clear picture. The Front Door module has:
1. URL path prefix stripping
2. WAF
3. Route rules
4. **NO CORS response headers configuration**

The key thing I notice is that there's **no `azurerm_cdn_frontdoor_rule_set` or rule that adds or preserves CORS headers**. Azure Front Door (CDN Standard SKU) has a known behavior where it can cache responses. When AFD caches a response, it may serve the cached response without the CORS headers that were returned by the origin for a different `Origin` request.

More specifically, **Azure Front Door Standard caches responses**, and when it does, it strips the `Vary: Origin` handling - meaning a cached response with `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://rnd.ixxdna.com` might be served to a request from `https://lexxi.ixxdna.com` or vice versa.
 
Hold on though - the CORS error is happening on a `change-password` endpoint, which would typically be a POST or PUT request, and those shouldn't be cached by AFD. Let me look more carefully at the route configuration and what backends are actually set up in the production Terraform to understand how the routing is structured.

##=============================================================================
## AZURE FRONT DOOR MODULE
##=============================================================================
## Provides global public access with managed HTTPS (*.azurefd.net certificate)
## Features:
## - Automatic HTTPS certificate management (no renewal needed)
## - WAF protection with OWASP ModSecurity rules
## - Global CDN with DDoS protection (layers 3-7)
## - HTTP → HTTPS automatic redirect
## - Health probes for backend monitoring
## Status: Replaces Application Gateway for lower cost ($6/month vs $32/month) and simplicity
##

module "front_door" {

  source = "../../modules/front-door"

  resource_group_name = azurerm_resource_group.rg.name
  environment         = var.environment
  resource_prefix     = local.resource_prefix
  tags                = local.tags

  # Front Door Configuration
  front_door_sku          = var.front_door_sku
  custom_domain_host_name = "api.ixxdna.com"

  # Backend Configuration - Multiple backends for Lexxi, RD, and Keycloak with path-based routing
  # NOTE: Route Keycloak over HTTPS to bypass HTTP-only proxy header issues
  backends = [
    {
      name              = "lexxi"
      fqdn              = azurerm_container_app.lexxi.ingress[0].fqdn
      port              = 80
      protocol          = "Http"
      path              = "/lexxi/*"
      health_path       = "/"
      strip_path_prefix = true
    },
    {
      name              = "rd"
      fqdn              = azurerm_container_app.rd.ingress[0].fqdn
      port              = 80
      protocol          = "Http"
      path              = "/rd/*"
      health_path       = "/"
      strip_path_prefix = true
    },
    {
      name             = "keycloak"
      fqdn             = azurerm_container_app.keycloak.ingress[0].fqdn
      port             = 443
      protocol         = "Https"
      path             = "/auth/*"
      health_path      = "/auth/realms/master/.well-known/openid-configuration"
      session_affinity = true
    }
  ]

  # WAF Configuration
  enable_waf = var.enable_front_door_waf
  waf_mode   = var.front_door_waf_mode

  depends_on = [
    azurerm_container_app.lexxi,
    azurerm_container_app.rd,
    azurerm_container_app.keycloak,
    module.networking
  ]
}



##=============================================================================
## STATIC WEB APP MODULE (lexxi)
##=============================================================================
## Azure Static Web App for Angular frontend deployment
## Features:
## - Free tier (no cost)
## - GitHub Actions CI/CD integration
## - Managed HTTPS certificate (*.azurestaticapps.net)
## - Global CDN distribution
## - API proxy to backend via staticwebapp.config.json
##
## Deployment:
## - Automatically deployed from main branch
## - Build command: ng build
## - Build output: dist/
## - GitHub Enterprise authentication via PAT token
##
## Access:
## - Frontend: https://<app-name>.azurestaticapps.net
## - API: Proxied through Static Web App to Front Door endpoint
##

module "static_web_app_lexxi" {

  source = "../../modules/static-web-app"

  resource_group_name = azurerm_resource_group.rg.name
  location            = var.azure_region
  environment         = var.environment
  resource_prefix     = local.resource_prefix
  app_name            = "lexxis"

  # GitHub Configuration
  github_repo_url = var.lexxi_github_repo_url
  github_token    = var.github_token
  github_branch   = var.lexxi_github_branch

  # Build Configuration
  app_location    = var.swa_app_location
  output_location = var.swa_output_location
  build_command   = var.swa_build_command

  # API Configuration
  api_endpoint = module.front_door.endpoint_fqdn
  api_key      = ""

  sku_tier = "Free"

Now I see the full picture. 

The static web app has:
```
api_endpoint = module.front_door.endpoint_fqdn
```

This means the frontend is configured to call the API via the Front Door endpoint FQDN (something like `intellxxdna-prod-fd-endpoint.azurefd.net`), NOT via the custom domain `api.ixxdna.com`.

BUT the `CORS_ALLOW_ORIGINS` in the backend is set to `"https://lexxi.ixxdna.com,https://rnd.ixxdna.com"` - the custom domains for the static web apps.


So the actual flow is: the frontend at `https://rnd.ixxdna.com` makes requests to the API, and the browser sends the `Origin` header as `https://rnd.ixxdna.com`, but the backend's CORS configuration only allows those custom domains, not the Front Door endpoint that the frontend is actually calling. I need to trace through what's actually happening when the frontend makes a request. The browser at `rnd.ixxdna.com` is calling the API, and I need to figure out what `Origin` header the backend actually sees when the request goes through the Static Web App proxy to Azure Front Door to the container app.

So the flow is: the browser sends the request with `Origin: https://rnd.ixxdna.com`, it hits AFD at the custom domain, AFD forwards it to the RD container while preserving that origin header along with its own forwarding headers. The FastAPI app then checks that origin against its allowed list and responds with the matching CORS header.

Now I'm thinking about what happens after AFD gets that response back. AFD could be caching responses and potentially stripping CORS headers when serving cached content, or it might cache a response with one origin's CORS header and then serve it to requests from different origins. But actually, POST requests shouldn't be cached by default, so that's probably not the issue here.

Let me think about this differently—maybe AFD's WAF is blocking or mishandling the preflight OPTIONS request. If the OPTIONS request gets blocked before it even reaches the backend, the browser would never attempt the actual POST request.

But wait, the user said the operation actually succeeds—the container returns 200 and the update happens. So the request is definitely getting through and being processed. The browser is just showing a CORS error even though the server already did the work.

That means this is likely a simple request that doesn't require preflight. The browser sends the request directly, the server processes it and returns 200, but the response is missing the `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` header or it doesn't match the requesting origin. The server already completed the operation, but the browser blocks the response from being read due to the missing CORS header.

Or it could be a preflight situation where the OPTIONS request succeeds with proper CORS headers, but then the actual POST response is missing those headers—either because the backend CORS middleware isn't applying them consistently, or Azure Front Door is stripping them from the actual response while leaving them on the OPTIONS response. I should also check if AFD has any caching rules that might be interfering with how responses are being handled.

Let me reconsider the protocol mismatch angle—if AFD is forwarding to the backend over HTTP while the browser is making requests from HTTPS, the Origin header would still be `https://rnd.ixxdna.com`, so the CORS check should work fine. But I need to understand what `strip_path_prefix = true` is actually doing with the `/rd/` routing rule and how that affects the request reaching the backend.

Actually, I should look at whether the container app's ingress IP allowlist is blocking the OPTIONS preflight requests somehow, even though both the preflight and actual request should be going through AFD. Let me examine the ingress configuration more carefully to see how the IP restriction actually works.

Wait, let me take a step back and think about this more practically. The core issue is a CORS error in prod but not dev for that endpoint, and the main difference I'm seeing is that prod's CORS allowlist only includes two origins while dev has six. The frontend at `https://rnd.ixxdna.com` is definitely in the prod list, so that's not the issue—but maybe there's something about how the CORS headers are being set or validated that's different between the two environments.

Actually, I'm realizing the user might be hitting this endpoint from somewhere unexpected. If they're accessing it from a Keycloak account management page at `https://api.ixxdna.com/auth/...`, that would be a same-origin request and wouldn't trigger CORS at all. But if they're coming from a legacy .NET portal at a different domain like `https://app.ixxdna.com` or `https://portal.ixxdna.com`, that origin wouldn't be in the prod CORS allowlist, which would explain the error. The user's message about running the URL directly in the browser is a bit ambiguous—I need to clarify where the actual request is originating from. in the allowed origins list, which would cause a CORS error. So the issue might be that the change-password endpoint is being called from a legacy portal URL that hasn't been added to the CORS configuration yet.

I see the full picture now. Let me think about the problem more carefully.

The RD container has:
- `CORS_ALLOW_ORIGINS = "https://lexxi.ixxdna.com,https://rnd.ixxdna.com"`
- Ingress only allows Azure Front Door backend IPs
- `allow_insecure_connections = true`

The key issue I notice: the ingress has `allow_insecure_connections = true` but this means it allows HTTP. The container app is behind AFD, which forces HTTPS.

Now, the actual CORS issue...


Wait, I'm reconsidering this. If the user is accessing the endpoint directly through the browser address bar, CORS wouldn't even be a factor—that only applies to cross-origin requests from JavaScript. But if they're calling it from a web app on a different domain that's not in the allowed list, that would trigger CORS. Let me think about whether the change-password functionality might be hosted on a domain that's not included in the CORS configuration.

Actually, looking at that commit about migrating from SAML to OIDC—there's likely a legacy .NET portal involved. The password change could be initiated from that portal, which runs on a different URL that isn't whitelisted in production. In dev, the CORS list is broader and includes localhost, so testing locally works fine. But in production, the legacy portal's actual domain probably isn't in the allowed list, which would explain why it fails there but works in dev.

So I need to track down what the legacy portal's URL actually is—I should check the recent Keycloak commits or the production addon configuration to find that domain.

1→##
     2→## Dev Environment - Main Configuration
     3→## Author: Raul Peña (rpena@shokworks.io)
     4→## Purpose: Orchestrates all infrastructure modules for the development environment
     5→##
     6→## Environment: Development (cost-optimized, single-region)
     7→##
     8→## Modules & Resources Deployed:
     9→## - Keycloak: Authentication and identity management
    10→
    11→
    12→##=============================================================================
    13→## CURRENT AZURE CONTEXT
    14→##=============================================================================
    15→## Get current subscription and tenant information for identity and RBAC configuration
    16→
    17→data "azurerm_client_config" "current" {}
    18→
    19→
    20→##=============================================================================
    21→## DATA: KEYCLOAK AUTHENTICATION FLOW - DEFAULT BROWSER
    22→##=============================================================================
    23→## Fetches the built-in default "browser" authentication flow.
    24→## This flow is the standard method used when users authenticate via a web browser.
    25→## It is used as a base to retrieve and modify specific execution steps within it.
    26→##
    27→
    28→data "keycloak_authentication_flow" "default_browser" {
    29→
    30→  realm_id = keycloak_realm.main.id
    31→  alias    = "browser"
    32→}
    33→
    34→
    35→##=============================================================================
    36→## DATA: KEYCLOAK AUTHENTICATION EXECUTION - DEFAULT IDP REDIRECTOR
    37→##=============================================================================
    38→## Fetches the built-in "identity-provider-redirector" execution step from the 
    39→## default browser flow. This reference is required to override its configuration
    40→## (e.g., configuring automatic redirection to the legacy IdP).
    41→##
    42→
    43→data "keycloak_authentication_execution" "default_idp_redirector" {
    44→
    45→  realm_id          = keycloak_realm.main.id
    46→  parent_flow_alias = data.keycloak_authentication_flow.default_browser.alias
    47→  provider_id       = "identity-provider-redirector"
    48→}
    49→
    50→
    51→##=============================================================================
    52→## KEYCLOAK RESOURCES
    53→##=============================================================================
    54→
    55→##=============================================================================
    56→## KEYCLOAK REALM - MAIN
    57→##=============================================================================
    58→## Creates the primary realm for Lexxi Keycloak authentication
    59→## Realms are isolated authentication domains where users, clients, and roles exist
    60→##
    61→
    62→resource "keycloak_realm" "main" {
    63→
    64→  realm        = "IntellxxDNA"
    65→  enabled      = true
    66→  display_name = "IntellxxDNA Realm"
    67→
    68→  # Password policy: minimum 12 chars, 1 uppercase, 1 lowercase, 1 digit, 1 special char
    69→  password_policy = "length(12) and upperCase(1) and lowerCase(1) and digits(1) and specialChars(1)"
    70→}
    71→
    72→
    73→##=============================================================================
    74→## KEYCLOAK OPENID CLIENT - IXXD LEGACY
    75→##=============================================================================
    76→## Creates an OpenID Connect (OIDC) confidential client for the Legacy application.
    77→## This client is used to integrate the existing .NET application with Keycloak
    78→## for centralized authentication and single sign-on (SSO).
    79→##
    80→## Features:
    81→## - Confidential client (requires a client secret)
    82→## - Standard flow (Authorization Code Flow) enabled
    83→## - Strict redirect URIs and web origins targeting the legacy app endpoint
    84→##
    85→
    86→resource "keycloak_openid_client" "ixxd-legacy" {
    87→
    88→  access_type                                = "CONFIDENTIAL"
    89→  backchannel_logout_revoke_offline_sessions = false
    90→  backchannel_logout_session_required        = true
    91→  backchannel_logout_url                     = null
    92→  client_authenticator_type                  = "client-secret"
    93→  client_id                                  = "ixxd-legacy"
    94→  client_secret                              = null # sensitive
    95→  consent_required                           = false
    96→  description                                = "IXXD Legacy Application"
    97→  direct_access_grants_enabled               = false
    98→  display_on_consent_screen                  = false
    99→  enabled                                    = true
   100→  extra_config                               = {}
   101→  frontchannel_logout_enabled                = true
   102→  frontchannel_logout_url                    = null
   103→  full_scope_allowed                         = true
   104→  implicit_flow_enabled                      = false
   105→  import                                     = false
   106→  login_theme                                = null
   107→  name                                       = "IXXD Legacy"
   108→  oauth2_device_authorization_grant_enabled  = false
   109→  oauth2_device_code_lifespan                = null
   110→  oauth2_device_polling_interval             = null
   111→  pkce_code_challenge_method                 = null
   112→  realm_id                                   = keycloak_realm.main.id
   113→  root_url                                   = var.ixxd_legacy_client_root_url
   114→  service_accounts_enabled                   = false
   115→  standard_flow_enabled                      = true
   116→  use_refresh_tokens                         = true
   117→  use_refresh_tokens_client_credentials      = false
   118→  valid_post_logout_redirect_uris            = ["${var.legacy_dotnet_idp_base_url}/logout*", "${var.ixxd_legacy_client_root_url}*"]
   119→  valid_redirect_uris                        = ["", "${var.ixxd_legacy_client_root_url}*"]
   120→  web_origins                                = ["+"]
   121→}
   122→
   123→
   124→##=============================================================================
   125→## KEYCLOAK OPENID CLIENT - LEXXI API
   126→##=============================================================================
   127→## Creates an OpenID Connect (OIDC) confidential client for the Lexxi API.
   128→## This client provides secure authentication for backend API services, allowing
   129→## validation of tokens issued by Keycloak and enabling machine-to-machine or
   130→## direct access flows if necessary.
   131→##
   132→## Features:
   133→## - Confidential client (requires a client secret)
   134→## - Standard flow (Authorization Code Flow) and Direct Access Grants enabled
   135→## - Supports wildcard redirect URIs and web origins (typical for dev environments)

   137→##
   138→
   139→resource "keycloak_openid_client" "lexi_api" {
   140→
   141→  access_type                                = "CONFIDENTIAL"
   142→  backchannel_logout_revoke_offline_sessions = false
   143→  backchannel_logout_session_required        = true
   144→  backchannel_logout_url                     = null
   145→  client_authenticator_type                  = "client-secret"
   146→  client_id                                  = "lexi_api"
   147→  client_secret                              = null # sensitive
   148→  consent_required                           = false
   149→  direct_access_grants_enabled               = true
   150→  display_on_consent_screen                  = false
   151→  enabled                                    = true
   152→  extra_config                               = {}
   153→  frontchannel_logout_enabled                = true
   154→  frontchannel_logout_url                    = null
   155→  full_scope_allowed                         = true
   156→  implicit_flow_enabled                      = false
   157→  import                                     = false
   158→  login_theme                                = null
   159→  oauth2_device_authorization_grant_enabled  = false
   160→  oauth2_device_code_lifespan                = null
   161→  oauth2_device_polling_interval             = null
   162→  pkce_code_challenge_method                 = null
   163→  realm_id                                   = keycloak_realm.main.id
   164→  service_accounts_enabled                   = false
   165→  standard_flow_enabled                      = true
   166→  use_refresh_tokens                         = false
   167→  use_refresh_tokens_client_credentials      = false
   168→  valid_post_logout_redirect_uris            = ["+"]
   169→  valid_redirect_uris                        = ["/*"]
   170→  web_origins                                = ["/*"]
   171→}
   172→
   173→
   174→##=============================================================================
   175→## KEYCLOAK AUTH EXECUTION CONFIG - IDP AUTO REDIRECT
   176→##=============================================================================
   177→## Configures the "identity-provider-redirector" step in the default browser 
   178→## authentication flow to automatically redirect users to the external Identity 
   179→## Provider (legacy-dotnet). 
   180→##
   181→## Features:
   182→## - Bypasses the standard Keycloak login screen for a seamless SSO experience
   183→## - Automatically routes unauthenticated users directly to the legacy .NET login
   184→##
   185→
   186→resource "keycloak_authentication_execution_config" "idp_auto_redirect" {
   187→
   188→  realm_id     = keycloak_realm.main.id
   189→  execution_id = data.keycloak_authentication_execution.default_idp_redirector.id
   190→  alias        = "auth-api-redirect"
   191→
   192→  config = {
   193→    defaultProvider = "legacy-dotnet"
   194→  }
   195→}
   196→
   197→
   198→##=============================================================================
   199→## KEYCLOAK OIDC IDENTITY PROVIDER - LEGACY DOTNET
   200→##=============================================================================
   201→## Configures the legacy .NET application as an external Identity Provider (IdP)
   202→## in Keycloak via OpenID Connect (OIDC). This allows users to authenticate
   203→## using their existing legacy credentials, with Keycloak acting as an identity broker.
   204→##
   205→## Features:
   206→## - Acts as an OIDC identity broker for the legacy app
   207→## - Maps to the authorization and token endpoints of the .NET application
   208→## - Supports backchannel logout integration
   209→## - Sync mode set to IMPORT to sync user identities into Keycloak upon login
   210→##

   212→resource "keycloak_oidc_identity_provider" "legacy-dotnet" {
   213→
   214→  accepts_prompt_none_forward_from_client = null
   215→  add_read_token_role_on_create           = false
   216→  alias                                   = "legacy-dotnet"
   217→  authenticate_by_default                 = false
   218→  authorization_url                       = "${var.legacy_dotnet_idp_base_url}/connect/authorize"
   219→  backchannel_supported                   = true

   222→  default_scopes                          = null
   223→  disable_user_info                       = false
   224→  display_name                            = "Log in with Legacy"
   225→  enabled                                 = true
   226→  extra_config                            = {}
   227→  first_broker_login_flow_alias           = null
   228→  gui_order                               = null
   229→  hide_on_login_page                      = false
   230→  issuer                                  = null
   231→  jwks_url                                = null
   232→  link_only                               = false
   233→  login_hint                              = "false"
   234→  logout_url                              = "${var.legacy_dotnet_idp_base_url}/backchannel-logout"
   235→  post_broker_login_flow_alias            = null
   236→  provider_id                             = null
   237→  realm                                   = keycloak_realm.main.id
   238→  store_token                             = false
   239→  sync_mode                               = "IMPORT"
   240→  token_url                               = "${var.legacy_dotnet_idp_base_url}/connect/token"
   241→  trust_email                             = false
   242→  ui_locales                              = false
   243→  user_info_url                           = null
   244→  validate_signature                      = false
   245→}

   285→
   286→
   287→
   288→##=============================================================================
   289→## KEYCLOAK OPENID CLIENT - LEXXI WEB APP
   290→##=============================================================================
   291→## Registers the Lexxi frontend as a public OAuth2/OpenID Connect client
   292→## This client handles user authentication for the Lexxi single-page application
   293→##
   294→## Client Type: PUBLIC
   295→## Authentication Flow: Authorization Code Flow with PKCE (OAuth 2.0 standard)
   296→## Purpose: Secure browser-based authentication without exposing client secrets
   297→##
   298→## Security Notes:
   299→## - Public clients cannot securely store secrets (runs in user's browser)
   300→## - PKCE (RFC 7636) prevents authorization code interception attacks
   301→## - Redirect URIs must be explicitly whitelisted to prevent open redirects
   302→## - CORS origins limited to prevent unauthorized token requests
   303→##
   304→
   305→resource "keycloak_openid_client" "lexxi_web_app_client" {
   306→
   307→  realm_id  = keycloak_realm.main.id
   308→  client_id = "lexxi-web-app-client"
   309→  name      = "Lexxi Web App Client"
   310→  enabled   = true
   311→
   312→  ##=======================================================================
   313→  ## CLIENT AUTHENTICATION & FLOW SETTINGS
   314→  ##=======================================================================
   315→  ## access_type = "PUBLIC"
   316→  ##   - Indicates this is a public client (browser-based SPA)
   317→  ##   - Client cannot authenticate with a secret
   318→  ##   - Used for single-page applications, mobile apps, desktop clients
   319→  ##
   320→  ## standard_flow_enabled = true
   321→  ##   - Enables Authorization Code Flow (recommended for SPAs)
   322→  ##   - User redirects to Keycloak login → receives auth code → app exchanges for token
   323→  ##   - More secure than implicit flow (flow no longer recommended)
   324→  ##
   325→  access_type           = "PUBLIC"
   326→  standard_flow_enabled = true
   327→
   328→  ##=======================================================================
   329→  ## PKCE (PROOF KEY FOR AUTHORIZATION CODE EXCHANGE)
   330→  ##=======================================================================
   331→  ## pkce_code_challenge_method = "S256"
   332→  ##   - PKCE adds extra security layer to Authorization Code Flow
   333→  ##   - Prevents authorization code interception/replay attacks
   334→  ##   - Client generates random code_verifier → sends code_challenge (SHA256 hash)
   335→  ##   - When exchanging code for token, client proves it has original code_verifier
   336→  ##   - Mandatory security requirement for public clients in production (2026+ standards)
   337→  ##   - Options: "S256" (SHA256, recommended) or "plain" (not recommended)
   338→  ##
   339→  pkce_code_challenge_method = "S256"
   340→
   341→  ##=======================================================================
   342→  ## REDIRECT URIS (Authorization Code Response Destinations)
   343→  ##=======================================================================
   344→  ## valid_redirect_uris = [...]
   345→  ##   - Whitelisted URLs where Keycloak can redirect after user login
   346→  ##   - Prevents open redirect attacks (attacker cannot redirect to arbitrary URLs)
   347→  ##   - Must match exactly (protocol, domain, path) - wildcards allowed only on paths
   348→  ##   - Azure Static Web App: automatically generates *.azurestaticapps.net domain
   349→  ##   - Wildcard pattern: "https://domain/*" allows all paths under domain
   350→  ##
   351→  ##   Example redirects that will be allowed:
   352→  ##   - https://myapp.azurestaticapps.net/
   353→  ##   - https://myapp.azurestaticapps.net/dashboard
   354→  ##   - https://myapp.azurestaticapps.net/auth/callback
   355→  ##
   356→  valid_redirect_uris = [
   357→    "https://localhost/*"
   358→  ]
   359→
   360→  ##=======================================================================
   361→  ## WEB ORIGINS (CORS - Cross-Origin Resource Sharing)
   362→  ##=======================================================================
   363→  ## web_origins = [...]
   364→  ##   - Whitelisted origins allowed to make CORS requests to Keycloak
   365→  ##   - Prevents malicious websites from stealing user tokens
   366→  ##   - Only allows JavaScript from known origins to access Keycloak tokens
   367→  ##   - Must omit path (CORS is enforced at origin level, not path level)
   368→  ##   - Typically same as your frontend domain
   369→  ##
   370→  ##   Security Impact:
   371→  ##   - Without CORS restriction: Any website can make requests to Keycloak on user's behalf
   372→  ##   - With CORS restriction: Only your frontend domain can access user tokens/endpoints
   373→  ##
   374→  web_origins = [
   375→    "https://localhost"
   376→  ]
   377→
   378→  depends_on = [
   379→    keycloak_realm.main
   380→  ]
   381→}
   382→
   383→##=============================================================================
   384→## KEYCLOAK OPENID CLIENT - LEXXI BACKEND API
   385→##=============================================================================
   386→## Registers the Lexxi backend API as a confidential OAuth2/OpenID Connect client
   387→## This client represents server-to-server or internal API communication
   388→##
   389→## Client Type: CONFIDENTIAL (Backend API)
   390→## Authentication: Client credentials flow (for service-to-service auth)
   391→## Purpose: Secure API-to-API communication with client secret authentication
   392→##
   393→## Security Notes:
   394→## - Confidential clients can securely store secrets (runs on secure server)
   395→## - Uses client_id + client_secret for authentication (like Basic Auth)
   396→## - Service accounts enable service-to-service authentication
   397→## - Tokens include 'aud' (audience) claim to restrict token usage to specific APIs
   398→##
   399→## Communication Pattern:
   400→## 1. Lexxi API → Keycloak: POST /token with client_id + client_secret
   401→## 2. Keycloak → Lexxi API: Returns JWT with audience claim
   402→## 3. Frontend/other services: Can verify token is meant for this API via 'aud' claim
   403→##
   404→
   405→resource "keycloak_openid_client" "lexxi_backend_api" {
   406→
   407→  realm_id  = keycloak_realm.main.id
   408→  client_id = "lexxi-backend-api-client"
   409→  name      = "Lexxi Backend API Client"
   410→  enabled   = true
   411→
   412→  ##=======================================================================
   413→  ## CLIENT AUTHENTICATION SETTINGS
   414→  ##=======================================================================
   415→  ## access_type = "CONFIDENTIAL"
   416→  ##   - Indicates this is a confidential client (backend server)
   417→  ##   - Client can securely store and use a secret
   418→  ##   - Uses client credentials or authorization code + secret for auth
   419→  ##
   420→  ## service_accounts_enabled = true
   421→  ##   - Allows API to authenticate on its own (service-to-service auth)
   422→  ##   - Without this: API can only be called with user-delegated tokens
   423→  ##   - With this: API can obtain tokens directly for internal operations
   424→  ##   - Use case: Batch jobs, scheduled tasks, inter-service calls
   425→  ##
   426→  access_type              = "CONFIDENTIAL"
   427→  service_accounts_enabled = true
   428→
   429→  depends_on = [
   430→    keycloak_openid_client.lexxi_web_app_client
   431→  ]
   432→}
   433→
   434→
   435→##=============================================================================
   436→## KEYCLOAK OPENID AUDIENCE PROTOCOL MAPPER
   437→##=============================================================================
   438→## Adds the 'aud' (Audience) claim to JWT tokens for the Python API client
   439→## Audience claim restricts token usage to specific intended recipients
   440→##
   441→## What is the 'aud' Claim?
   442→## ├─ Standard OAuth2/OpenID Connect claim in JWT tokens
   443→## ├─ Specifies which API/service is intended to consume the token
   444→## ├─ Prevents token misuse (stolen token can't be used for wrong API)
   445→## └─ Example JWT payload: { aud: "python-backend-api", sub: "user123" }
   446→##
   447→## Security Flow:
   448→## 1. Frontend gets token: { aud: "python-backend-api", sub: "user123" }
   449→## 2. Frontend sends token to Python API
   450→## 3. Python API verifies: aud claim == "python-backend-api" ✓
   451→## 4. Token accepted and processed
   452→## 5. If attacker tries same token on different API:
   453→##    - That API checks: aud claim != "my-api" ✗
   454→##    - Token rejected (prevents cross-API token reuse)
   455→##
   456→## Mapper Type: aud (Audience) Claim Mapper
   457→## Maps to: Frontend client (angular_client) - specifies which client gets this mapper
   458→## Audience value: "lexxi-backend-api" - the target API identifier
   459→##
   460→
   461→
   462→
   463→
   464→##=============================================================================
   465→## KEYCLOAK OPENID AUDIENCE PROTOCOL MAPPER - LEXXI BACKEND API
   466→##=============================================================================
   467→## Injects the 'aud' (Audience) claim into JWT tokens issued to the Lexxi
   468→## Web App client, so the Lexxi Backend API can verify the token was intended
   469→## specifically for it.
   470→##
   471→## Why is this needed?
   472→## ├─ Without an audience claim, a token issued for any client could technically
   473→## │  be sent to any API — nothing stops token reuse across services
   474→## ├─ With this mapper, every token issued to the frontend includes:
   475→## │  "aud": "lexxi-backend-api"
   476→## └─ The Backend API validates this claim and rejects tokens meant for others
   477→##
   478→## Where it is attached:
   479→## ├─ Client: lexxi-web-app-client (the Angular SPA)
   480→## └─ Effect: every token the frontend obtains will carry the audience claim
   481→##
   482→## Security Flow:
   483→## 1. User logs in via the Angular SPA → Keycloak issues JWT
   484→## 2. JWT contains: { "aud": "lexxi-backend-api", "sub": "user-uuid", ... }
   485→## 3. Angular sends JWT to the Backend API
   486→## 4. Backend API checks: aud == "lexxi-backend-api" ✓ → request accepted
   487→## 5. If same token is sent to a different API:
   488→##    └─ That API checks: aud != "its-own-id" ✗ → request rejected
   489→##
   490→## Mapper Type: Audience (aud claim)
   491→## Attached to: lexxi-web-app-client
   492→## Audience value injected: "lexxi-backend-api"
   493→##
   494→
   495→resource "keycloak_openid_audience_protocol_mapper" "api_audience_mapper" {
   496→
   497→  realm_id  = keycloak_realm.main.id
   498→  client_id = keycloak_openid_client.lexxi_web_app_client.id
   499→  name      = "lexxi-backend-api-audience-mapper"
   500→
   501→  ##=======================================================================
   502→  ## AUDIENCE CONFIGURATION
   503→  ##=======================================================================
   504→  ## included_custom_audience = "lexxi-backend-api"
   505→  ##   - The audience value to include in JWT tokens
   506→  ##   - Must match the Lexxi API's expected audience
   507→  ##   - When frontend requests token, it will include this audience claim
   508→  ##   - Lexxi API can then verify the token is intended for it
   509→  ##
   510→  ##   Example JWT:
   511→  ##   {
   512→  ##     "iss": "https://keycloak.example.com/auth/realms/lexxi",
   513→  ##     "sub": "user-uuid",
   514→  ##     "aud": "lexxi-backend-api",     ← This claim was added by mapper
   515→  ##     "iat": 1609459200,
   516→  ##     "exp": 1609462800
   517→  ##   }
   518→  ##
   519→  included_custom_audience = "lexxi-backend-api"
   520→
   521→  depends_on = [
   522→    keycloak_openid_client.lexxi_web_app_client,
   523→    keycloak_openid_client.lexxi_backend_api
   524→  ]
   525→}
   526→
